summary: SAM proposes a promptable segmentation foundation model trained on a 1.1B-mask, 11M-image dataset (SA-1B) collected via a model-in-the-loop data engine, yielding strong zero-shot transfer across 23 segmentation benchmarks.
recommendationReason: Genuinely new paradigm for segmentation: promptable task formulation, ambiguity-aware multi-mask prediction, and a scale of supervision (1.1B masks) unprecedented in segmentation.
keyContribution: Promptable segmentation as a pre-training objective, paired with a data engine that bootstraps a 1.1B-mask dataset, enabling zero-shot transfer to many downstream segmentation tasks via prompt engineering.
methodologySummary: SAM is composed of an MAE-pretrained ViT-H image encoder, a lightweight prompt encoder (points/boxes/text/masks via positional + CLIP embeddings), and a two-layer transformer mask decoder that predicts three masks per prompt to handle ambiguity. Training uses a focal+dice loss (20:1) over interactively simulated prompts on the SA-1B dataset built by a three-stage (assisted-manual → semi-automatic → fully-automatic) data engine.
strengths: Promptable segmentation task and ambiguity-aware multi-mask head form a clean, reusable foundation-model interface for segmentation.
SA-1B is 400× larger than the next-largest segmentation dataset (Open Images V5) and supported by a careful human-quality verification (94% of pairs >90% IoU).
Zero-shot evaluation on 23 datasets plus four downstream tasks (edge detection, object proposals, instance seg, text-to-mask) gives strong evidence of transfer.
Fairness analysis across perceived gender, age, and skin tone is reported with 95% CIs, and dataset/model cards are released.
Released model + dataset under permissive license, with web demo enabling reproducibility.
weaknesses: Heavy ViT-H image encoder limits real-time use to amortized settings (image embedding precomputed); not real-time on full pipelines.
Text-to-mask path is a proof-of-concept and trains via CLIP image embeddings, not native text supervision.
Edge detection precision is biased (over-predicts edges) because no edge-suppression supervision exists in SA-1B.
On COCO, fully-supervised ViTDet still outperforms SAM on standard metrics — the human-rating advantage is mainly visible on higher-quality LVIS masks.
rankingExplanation: PDF analysis confirms novelty=22 (new task formulation + foundation-model paradigm for segmentation), methodologicalRigor=20 (ambiguity-aware design, three-stage data engine, RAI analysis), experimentalQuality=18 (23-dataset zero-shot + four downstream tasks + human study). Top-1 by total (86).

summary: EfficientFormerV2 revisits ViT design choices for mobile devices and proposes a fine-grained joint NAS over depth, width, and FFN expansion ratios that jointly optimizes a Mobile Efficiency Score (MES), achieving MobileNetV2-level latency at 3.5% higher top-1 on ImageNet-1K.
recommendationReason: Solid execution of mobile-ViT engineering with multi-task validation (ImageNet, COCO, ADE20K) and on-device latency measurement. Novelty is incremental over EfficientFormer v1 and prior efficient-ViT NAS works.
keyContribution: Fine-grained per-block NAS over FFN expansion ratios alongside depth and width, with a joint MES objective that simultaneously optimizes latency and parameter count, applied to a unified-FFN supernet.
methodologySummary: Builds on EfficientFormer-L1 by unifying token mixers (drop residual local mixers, use depth-wise FFN globally), adding talking-head attention with locality, dual-path attention downsampling, and stride attention at higher resolutions. The supernet is pretrained with the slimmable-network sandwich rule, then a frontier-search algorithm jointly minimizes ΔAccuracy/ΔMES over depth/width/expansion actions.
strengths: MES objective is a clean joint formulation of size and latency that better matches mobile-deployment reality than searching over a single metric.
Latency measured on three platforms (iPhone 12 NPU, Pixel 6 CPU, A100 GPU), reducing single-device bias.
Downstream-task validation on COCO detection and ADE20K segmentation, not just ImageNet classification.
Search algorithm ablation against random search and the EfficientFormer v1 search method (Tab. 9) shows search contributes ~0.4% beyond a strong manual baseline.
weaknesses: Headline 3.5% gain over MobileNetV2 v1.0 is partly attributable to the heavier supernet pretraining recipe, not search alone.
Architectural changes (unified FFN, locality in V, talking head) are individually small and prior-art-adjacent — Sec. 3 reads as engineering accumulation rather than a single new idea.
No comparison against more recent SSM- or Mamba-based mobile backbones; baselines are all from the 2022 efficient-ViT cohort.
rankingExplanation: PDF confirms incremental nature: novelty=9 (FFN-ratio search + minor block tweaks over v1), methodologicalRigor=17 (proper supernet + sandwich rule + multi-platform latency), experimentalQuality=15 (three benchmarks, three hardware platforms, real ablations). Total 61 → STORE_ONLY (just below the 65 RECOMMEND threshold).

summary: ViT-22B presents a recipe for stably training a 22B-parameter Vision Transformer (parallel attention/MLP, QK normalization, omitted biases) and evaluates the resulting frozen-feature model across classification, OOD, segmentation, depth, video, fairness, robustness, and distillation tasks.
recommendationReason: Substantial scaling and engineering achievement with broad downstream evaluation, but the core architectural changes are inherited from PaLM and prior scaling literature; algorithmic novelty is modest.
keyContribution: Three minimal architectural modifications — parallel layers, query/key LayerNorm, and bias removal on QKV/LayerNorm — that, combined with explicit JAX-xmap sharding, enable stable training of a 22B-parameter dense ViT achieving 54.9% MFU on TPUv4.
methodologySummary: Architecturally a parallel-block ViT with QK-norm to control attention-logit growth observed at the 8B scale; trained on a 4B-image extension of JFT for 177k steps with multi-label sigmoid cross-entropy. The implementation uses jax.xmap with asynchronous parallel linear operations to overlap communication and computation across a 2D mesh, with row/column sharding alternated to control reduce-scatter cost.
strengths: QK normalization is a clean, well-documented intervention: Fig. 1 shows divergent attention-logit growth without it, validated on an 8B-scale ablation.
JAX-xmap engineering hits 54.9% MFU on TPUv4, materially above PaLM's reported 46.2% — a real systems contribution.

Exceptionally broad downstream evaluation: ImageNet variants, ObjectNet, OOD robustness, semantic segmentation (ADE20K), monocular depth (Waymo), video classification (Kinetics-400, Moments in Time), CelebA fairness, calibration, distillation.
Reports state-of-the-art shape bias (87% vs human 96%) and distillation gains over prior ViT-L checkpoints.
Fairness experiments (Fig. 7) actually show ViT-22B reduces ACC gap across CelebA subgroups, not just average accuracy improvements.
weaknesses: Parallel attention/MLP is taken directly from PaLM; QK norm is taken from Gilmer et al. 2023; bias removal is also from PaLM. The combined recipe is engineering, not a new method.
Most downstream evaluations use a frozen-feature linear probe — this is the right scaling experiment but limits algorithmic insight beyond 'features improve with scale'.
No comparison against sparse-MoE vision models (SwitchViT/Pathways) at matched compute; the claim that dense scaling is the path forward is not contrasted with the obvious sparse alternative.
rankingExplanation: PDF analysis tightens novelty=11 (engineering recipe; architectural changes are derivative). methodologicalRigor=19 from training-stability ablations + MFU engineering. experimentalQuality=17 from breadth across 8+ task families. authorInstitutionReputation=14 — Google Research, all flagship affiliations (median rule yields a high but consistent score). Total 73 → RECOMMEND.

summary: SeaThru-NeRF integrates the SeaThru wideband image-formation model into NeRF rendering, decomposing each ray into separate object and medium components so the scene and the scattering/absorbing medium are jointly recovered, enabling photorealistic novel-view synthesis and medium-free reconstruction in real underwater scenes.
recommendationReason: Genuinely opens NeRF to scattering media via a physics-grounded rendering equation, with quantitative gains over MIP-NeRF360, NeRF-W, and NeRFReN on real Red-Sea/Curaçao/Panama footage from a small lab.
keyContribution: A NeRF rendering model in scattering media that splits each ray's color into object and medium contributions with separate per-color-channel medium parameters (σ^attn, σ^bs, c^med), enabling restoration of the clear scene and recovery of medium properties from posed underwater images.
methodologySummary: Starts from NeRF's volume-rendering equation and the SeaThru underwater image-formation model, derives discretized rendering equations that splits transmittance into 'attenuation' (T_i^obj × exp(-σ^attn s_i)) and 'backscatter' branches with different medium densities. The architecture has a position-only MLP for object density/color and a direction-only Medium MLP for medium parameters; training adds a propagation prior and a Laplacian-mixture occupancy prior to L_recon.
strengths: Physics-grounded ray model is more principled than prior NeRF-W / NeRFReN extensions for underwater appearance — Eq. 22's medium decomposition is directly tied to the SeaThru image formation model.

Quantitative comparison on three real underwater scenes (Red Sea, Curaçao, Panama) shows ~0.8 dB PSNR improvement over MIP-NeRF360 on novel views, with stronger gains in 'far' regions where scattering dominates.
Ablations on number of medium parameters (1 vs. 3 vs. 6) and rendering-equation choice (intermediate Eq. 13–14 vs. final Eq. 22) directly support the architectural decisions.
Even on synthetic clean (LLFF Fern in air) the method is competitive with MIP-NeRF360 baseline (Fig. 4), showing the medium model degrades gracefully when no medium is present.
Code, data, and dataset of underwater scenes released — substantive contribution to a niche but real domain.
weaknesses: Only three real scenes (sample sizes 18–20 images each); broader evaluation across water types and depths would strengthen the empirical claim.
Camera poses extracted by Agisoft/SfM are unreliable in low-visibility water — the paper notes this but does not fully quantify the failure regime.
The model assumes opaque objects with σ^obj ≫ σ^med; semi-transparent or strongly forward-scattering objects (jellyfish, kelp) likely fail.
Limitation section acknowledges no transient effects (flickering, caustics) — these are common in real underwater scenes.
rankingExplanation: PDF confirms strong novelty=19 (first principled NeRF-in-medium formulation). methodologicalRigor=17 from the derivation + ablations. experimentalQuality=15 — solid real-world results but the three-scene sample is small. authorInstitutionReputation=8 — University of Haifa + IUI Eilat, deliberately not a flagship roster, which is the F4 anti-pattern signal. Total 71 → RECOMMEND, confirming unknown-author papers can clear the floor.

summary: A qualitative report cataloguing GPT-4V's behavior on hand-curated multimodal samples, organized by task family and prompting style, framed as preliminary exploration of large multimodal models.
recommendationReason: No quantitative methodology, no benchmarks, and sweeping language ('unprecedented ability', 'powerful generalist system') unsupported by measured results. Useful as a probe of GPT-4V capabilities but not as a primary CV research contribution.
rankingExplanation: Stage 2 PDF analysis was attempted but the PDF (45.6 MB) exceeds the 32 MB skill cap; the record falls back to UNAVAILABLE with Stage-1 scoring preserved. From the abstract alone: novelty=5 (analyzes an existing third-party model), methodologicalRigor=6 (qualitative samples only), experimentalQuality=4 (no benchmarks). Bottom-1 by total (30) → LOW_QUALITY, confirming the F5 hype-driven anti-pattern.
